walkthrough.title: Gather Your Crew
walkthrough.text: Welcome to Monikers! First things first, let's split into two teams. You need at least four players total, and the more the merrier. Try to make the teams as even as possible, but if one team has an extra person, that's totally fine.

walkthrough.title: Deal the Cards
walkthrough.text: Now grab the big deck of name cards and give it a good shuffle. Deal eight cards face down to every player. Each card has a name or character on it, a funny little description, and a point value from one to three. Go ahead and look at your cards, but keep them secret from everyone else.

walkthrough.title: Draft Your Favorites
walkthrough.text: Here's your first fun decision. Look through your eight cards and pick five that you want to put into the game. Keep cards you actually know and can describe, act out, or boil down to one word. Discard the other three face down. Don't overthink it, just pick the ones that make you smile or that you think your team will recognize.

walkthrough.title: Build the Shared Deck
walkthrough.text: Collect everyone's five chosen cards and shuffle them all together into one big shared deck. This is the deck both teams will use for all three rounds. Set it face down in the center of the table where everyone can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Play Area
walkthrough.text: Set the sixty-second sand timer where both teams can see it. Each team needs a spot to pile up the cards they guess correctly. That's their score pile. Pick which team goes first by any method you like, such as a coin flip, rock-paper-scissors, or whoever has the best celebrity impression.

summary.title: Gather Your Crew
bullets: Split into 2 teams, as even as possible
Minimum 4 players, recommended 8
Uneven teams are fine — no penalty

summary.title: Deal the Cards
bullets: Shuffle the 440-card name deck
Deal 8 cards face-down to each player
Cards show a name, description, and point value (1-3)

summary.title: Draft Your Favorites
bullets: Each player secretly picks 5 cards to keep
Discard the other 3 face-down
Pick cards you know and can describe/act out

summary.title: Build the Shared Deck
bullets: Combine all kept cards into one shuffled deck
Both teams use this same deck for all 3 rounds
Place face-down in center of table

summary.title: Prepare the Play Area
bullets: Place 60-second sand timer centrally
Each team needs a score pile area
Choose starting team by any method

walkthrough.title: The Three Rounds
walkthrough.text: Monikers is played over three rounds, and here's the twist that makes it brilliant. You use the exact same deck of cards in every round, but the rules for giving clues get harder each time. Round one lets you say anything. Round two gives you just one word. Round three is pure charades. Because the cards repeat, the clues and jokes from earlier rounds become everyone's shared language.

walkthrough.title: How a Turn Works
walkthrough.text: When it's your turn to give clues, flip the sand timer and start drawing cards from the top of the deck. Give clues to your teammates according to the current round's rules. Your teammates shout out guesses as fast as they can, and there's no penalty for wrong guesses. When someone gets it right, toss that card into your team's score pile and grab the next card immediately.

walkthrough.title: Skipping and Timer
walkthrough.text: If you're stuck on a card, you can skip it. Put it on the bottom of the deck and draw a new one. When the timer runs out, your turn is over. Any card you were in the middle of cluing goes back into the deck. Then the other team takes their turn with a new clue-giver.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Describe It
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about what you can actually do in each round. In Round one, you can use any words, phrases, or sentences you want to describe the name on the card. You can talk as much as you want and gesture all you like. The only restriction is that you cannot say any part of the actual name on the card. This round is all about creating memorable descriptions that your team will remember later.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: One Word Only
walkthrough.text: Round two cranks up the challenge. You get exactly one word as your clue. Say your single word and then you must stop talking. You can use gestures, body language, and facial expressions to supplement your one word, but no additional words or sounds. You still cannot say any part of the name on the card. The magic here is that your one word usually references something from Round one that your team will remember.

walkthrough.title: Round Three: Charades Only
walkthrough.text: Round three is pure physical performance. No words, no sounds, no humming, no sound effects. You can only use gestures, body movement, and facial expressions. This is where all those descriptions and associations from Rounds one and two pay off. A simple gesture can trigger a whole chain of memories from earlier rounds, and that's what makes Monikers so hilarious.

walkthrough.title: When a Round Ends
walkthrough.text: A round ends when the last card in the deck has been guessed. It does not matter which team guesses it. Once the deck is empty, both teams collect all the scored cards from their piles, count up the point values, and record their scores for that round. Then gather every single card back together, reshuffle them into one deck, and start the next round.

walkthrough.title: Scoring and Winning
walkthrough.text: Each card is worth its printed point value: one, two, or three points. Points accumulate across all three rounds, so a card your team nails in all three rounds earns its value three separate times. After Round three, each team totals their points from all three rounds. The team with the highest total wins! In case of a tie, celebrate together because you both crushed it.

summary.title: The Three Rounds
bullets: 3 rounds using the same deck of cards each time
Clue rules get harder: describe → one word → charades
Shared knowledge from earlier rounds builds the fun

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Practice
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Monikers! In this practice tutorial, we'll walk through a few turns of each round with two example teams, the Stars and the Comets. We'll show you good moves, common mistakes, and how the magic of shared knowledge builds across rounds. Let's jump in!

walkthrough.title: The Draft Phase
walkthrough.text: Everyone has been dealt eight cards and needs to pick five. Let's watch Alex on the Stars make a smart pick. Alex sees "Darth Vader" (one point), "The Mona Lisa" (two points), and "Grigori Rasputin" (three points). Alex keeps Darth Vader and Mona Lisa because they're easy to describe and act out, and tosses Rasputin because half the group won't know who that is.

walkthrough.title: The Drafting Mistake
walkthrough.text: Meanwhile, Jordan on the Comets keeps all the three-point cards because they're worth the most. The problem is that these are obscure names nobody at the table recognizes. High point values mean nothing if your team can't guess them. Always draft cards your specific group will know, not just the highest-value ones.

walkthrough.title: Round One Begins
walkthrough.text: The deck is shuffled and the Stars go first. Alex flips the timer and draws a card: "Beyonce." Alex says, "She's married to Jay-Z, she sang Single Ladies, she told everyone to put a ring on it!" The team shouts "Beyonce!" and nails it in three seconds. Alex tosses it in the score pile and grabs the next card. Speed is the name of the game!

walkthrough.title: Creating Memorable Descriptions
walkthrough.text: Alex draws "Albert Einstein" next. Instead of just saying "famous scientist," Alex says, "Wild crazy hair, tongue sticking out in that famous photo, E equals MC squared!" This vivid description creates hooks the team will remember in later rounds. The phrase "crazy hair tongue out" will be gold in Round two when Alex only gets one word.

walkthrough.title: The Generic Description Mistake
walkthrough.text: Now it's the Comets' turn. Sam draws "Cleopatra" and says, "She was a famous historical woman who was really powerful." The team stares blankly. That description fits hundreds of people! Sam should have said something like, "Ancient Egyptian queen, asp snake, ruled with Marc Antony." Vague descriptions leave your team with nothing to hold onto in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: The Skip Decision
walkthrough.text: Alex draws a card and has absolutely no idea who this person is. After two seconds of hesitation, Alex skips it to the bottom of the deck and moves on. Good call! Spending fifteen seconds stumbling through a bad clue wastes precious time. But be careful not to skip too much. Every skip means that card stays in the deck and you'll have to face it again.

walkthrough.title: Round One Wraps Up
walkthrough.text: Teams keep alternating turns until the deck runs out. Both teams count their scored cards by point value and record Round one scores. Now here's the key moment: gather every single card back up, both teams' score piles, and shuffle them into one deck again. The exact same cards come back for Round two, and that's where the fun really starts.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: The One-Word Challenge
walkthrough.text: Round two begins! Alex draws "Beyonce" again and says just one word: "Ring." The Stars immediately shout "Beyonce!" because they remember "put a ring on it" from Round one. That's the shared knowledge mechanic in action. One word is all it takes when your team already has the association locked in.

walkthrough.title: The New Association Mistake
walkthrough.text: Sam draws "Cleopatra" again. Instead of referencing the Round one description, Sam says "Egypt" as the one word. Not bad, but the team hesitates because there are other Egypt-related cards in the deck. Sam should have said "asp" or "snake," referencing the specific Round one description. Always call back to what your team already heard, not a brand new association.

walkthrough.title: Round Three: Charades Time
walkthrough.text: Round three is where Monikers becomes absolutely hilarious. Alex draws "Beyonce" one more time. Without making a sound, Alex mimes putting a ring on a finger. The Stars erupt: "Beyonce!" Three rounds, three correct guesses, all building on that original "put a ring on it" description. This is why Round one descriptions matter so much.

walkthrough.title: The Panic Flail Mistake
walkthrough.text: A Comets clue-giver draws a card and immediately starts doing random frantic gestures with no plan. The team has no idea what's happening. Before you start acting, take one second to think about what association your team built in the previous rounds. Then act out that specific thing. A calm, deliberate gesture beats wild flailing every time.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: After Round three, both teams add up their points from all three rounds. The Stars scored 22 in Round one, 28 in Round two (faster because of shared knowledge), and 25 in Round three for a total of 75. The Comets scored 18, 15, and 12 for a total of 45. The Stars win! Notice how the Comets' score went down each round because their Round one descriptions were too vague to build on.

walkthrough.title: Before You Play
walkthrough.text:
You're ready for your first real game of Monikers! Remember the big lessons: draft cards your group actually knows, create vivid and specific descriptions in Round one, call back to those descriptions in Round two, and act out those same associations in Round three.

Check out General Tips for more strategy, and use the Appendix for a quick rules reference. Most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide any question during the game. Have a blast!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we dive into strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, giving generic descriptions like "famous person" in Round one. These give your team nothing to latch onto later. Second, drafting obscure cards just because they're worth three points. Third, spending too long on one card when you should skip and move on. Fourth, forgetting to watch the other team's turns. Paying attention to their clues helps if those cards cycle back to you.

walkthrough.title: Draft for Your Group
walkthrough.text: The single most impactful decision in Monikers happens before a single clue is given. When drafting, think about your specific group of friends, not the general population. Keep cards that your table will recognize. A one-point card everyone knows is worth way more than a three-point card nobody has heard of. Also consider Round three when you're drafting. If a name is easy to act out physically, keep it even if it's low value.

walkthrough.title: Round One: Plant the Seeds
walkthrough.text: Round one is not just about scoring points. It's about planting seeds for Rounds two and three. Every description you give should create a specific, memorable hook. Instead of "famous musician," say "purple suit, guitar, raining on stage." Instead of "movie character," say "red pill or blue pill, bullet time, sunglasses." The more vivid and unique your description, the easier it will be to trigger that memory with just one word or gesture later.

walkthrough.title: Speed vs. Detail Balance
walkthrough.text: There's a natural tension in Round one between being thorough and being fast. If you spend thirty seconds painting a perfect picture of one card, you've only gotten through two cards in your whole turn. But if you rush through with generic clues, you're hurting yourself in later rounds. The sweet spot is about five to eight seconds per card. Give two or three vivid details, get the guess, and move on. Practice finding that rhythm.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: Callback Strategy
walkthrough.text: In Round two, your one word should almost always reference your Round one description, not a completely new association. If you described Einstein as "crazy hair, tongue out, E equals MC squared," your Round two word should be "hair" or "tongue." Your team already has the connection built. A brand new word forces them to make a fresh association under pressure, which is much harder.

walkthrough.title: Round Three: Plan Before You Act
walkthrough.text: The biggest Round three mistake is starting to gesture before you have a plan. Take one full second to think about what your team's strongest association is for that card. Then commit to one clear gesture or action. If you described a character as riding a broomstick in Round one and said "broom" in Round two, just mime riding a broomstick. Don't try to add new information. Reinforce what already worked.

walkthrough.title: Watch the Other Team
walkthrough.text: Here's a tip that separates good teams from great teams. Pay attention when the other team is playing. You can hear their clues and see their charades. If a card gets skipped by the other team and ends up in your clue-giver's hands, your whole team might already know the answer from watching the other team's attempt. This is completely legal and very smart.

walkthrough.title: Rotate Clue-Givers Wisely
walkthrough.text: Everyone on the team should take turns being the clue-giver, but think about the rotation order. If someone on your team is naturally great at charades, save them for a Round three turn when the deck is still thick. If someone is a fast talker with great pop culture knowledge, let them go early in Round one to burn through cards quickly. Play to your teammates' strengths when deciding who goes next.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't give generic descriptions — 'famous person' gives no hook for later rounds
Don't draft obscure 3-point cards nobody knows
Don't spend 15 seconds on one card — skip after 2-3 seconds if stuck
Watch the other team's turns — you can use their clues legally

walkthrough.title: The Layered Description Technique
walkthrough.text: Advanced players don't just describe a card in Round one. They deliberately layer their description to set up all three rounds at once. When describing a name, include one fact for easy guessing, one distinctive physical trait for charades, and one single keyword that can be your Round two word. For example, for "Napoleon," you might say "short French emperor, hand tucked inside his jacket, Waterloo." The hand gesture is your Round three move, and "Waterloo" is your Round two word, all planted in one smooth description.

walkthrough.title: Strategic Skipping
walkthrough.text: Skipping is not just about being stuck. Smart clue-givers strategically skip cards they know will be easier in later rounds and burn through cards they want to lock in now. In Round one, you might skip a card that's easy to act out but hard to describe, knowing you'll crush it in Round three. In Rounds two and three, skip cards with weak associations and come back to them when the deck is thinner and the remaining cards are easier to distinguish.

walkthrough.title: Team Codebook Development
walkthrough.text: Great Monikers teams develop a shared codebook over the course of the game. If your team has an inside joke or a memorable moment from Round one, lean into it hard. When you described someone as "the one who eats weird stuff" and it got a huge laugh, that shared moment becomes a powerful retrieval cue. The emotional reaction makes it stickier in memory than any factual description. Experienced teams deliberately create these moments.

walkthrough.title: Deck Counting
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to how many cards are left in the deck and which cards have already been guessed. If you know there are only five cards remaining, your team has a much smaller pool to guess from. In Round three, when the deck is thin, a vague gesture might be enough because there are only three possible answers. Deck awareness lets you be less precise when precision isn't needed and save time.

walkthrough.title: Drafting for Round Three
walkthrough.text: Advanced drafters think backwards from Round three. They ask themselves, "Can I act this out with a clear gesture?" before keeping a card. Names with strong physical associations, like athletes, dancers, or characters known for a specific action, are gold. Abstract concepts and people known only for ideas are much harder. A card like "Michael Jackson" is a dream because one moonwalk gesture seals the deal in Round three.

walkthrough.title: The One-Word Power Move
walkthrough.text: In Round two, the best word is not always the most obvious one. Sometimes a seemingly random word works better because it's uniquely tied to your Round one description. If you described "The Statue of Liberty" as "big green lady holding a torch in New York Harbor," the word "green" is more distinctive than "statue" because "statue" could apply to multiple cards. Pick the word that narrows the field to exactly one card in your team's memory.

walkthrough.title: Opponent Awareness
walkthrough.text: Advanced play includes reading the other team. If you notice the other team struggling with a specific card, that card will keep cycling back into the deck. When it lands in your hands, you can prepare for it. Also, listen carefully to how the other team describes cards. Their descriptions become part of the shared knowledge too, and your team can use those associations just as effectively in later rounds.

walkthrough.title: Time Management Across Rounds
walkthrough.text: As rounds progress, your average time per card should decrease because of shared knowledge. In Round one, aim for five to eight seconds per card. In Round two, aim for two to four seconds. In Round three, aim for one to three seconds. If your Round three times are slower than Round one, your descriptions weren't memorable enough. Track your pacing mentally and adjust your approach for the next game.

bullets: Layer Round 1 descriptions: fact + physical trait + keyword
Plant your Round 2 word and Round 3 gesture in the same description
Napoleon: 'short French emperor, hand in jacket, Waterloo'

summary.title: Strategic Skipping
bullets: Skip cards that are easier in later rounds, lock in hard-to-act cards now
Round 1: skip actable but hard-to-describe cards
Rounds 2-3: skip weak associations, revisit when deck is thinner

entries.definition: Use any words, phrases, or sentences to describe the name on the card. No restrictions on how much you say. You may gesture freely. You cannot say any part of the name on the card.
Say exactly one word as your clue, then stop talking. Gestures and body language are allowed after your word. You cannot say any part of the name on the card.
No words, no sounds, no humming, no sound effects. Use only gestures, body movement, and facial expressions to communicate the name on the card.

Each card is worth 1, 2, or 3 points based on difficulty. One-point cards are well-known names, two-point cards are moderately recognizable, and three-point cards are obscure. Points are scored each time a card is correctly guessed in any round.

If stuck on a card, place it on the bottom of the deck and draw a new one. No point penalty, but the card stays in the deck and must be guessed eventually. Over-skipping wastes time without clearing cards.
Each turn lasts 60 seconds, measured by the sand timer. When time runs out, any card currently being clued goes back into the deck. The next team then takes their turn.
Within each team, players take turns being the clue-giver. Rotate after each turn so everyone gets a chance. The order can be strategic — fast talkers early in Round 1, strong performers in Round 3.
Both teams use the exact same deck of cards in all three rounds. After each round, all scored cards are gathered from both teams, reshuffled, and used again. This repetition creates shared knowledge.

The central mechanic of Monikers. Because the same cards repeat across three rounds, descriptions from Round 1 create associations that carry into Rounds 2 and 3. This is why vivid, specific Round 1 descriptions are critical.

Each player receives 8 cards and keeps 5 to put into the shared deck. Draft cards your group will recognize and that are easy to describe, reduce to one word, and act out. Discard the other 3 face-down.
Split into two roughly equal teams. Teams of 3-8 per side work well. Uneven teams play normally with no handicap. Players may sit together or intermixed.
The clue-giver may never say any part of the name printed on the card in any round. This applies to all three rounds. Spelling out the name is also prohibited.

Each team's pile of correctly guessed cards. At the end of each round, count the printed point values of all cards in your score pile. Points from all three rounds are added together for the final score.
